Requires licensed facilities storing clotting factors to maintain records and notify recipients of contamination.

label: Segment 1
summary: Mandates developing a DoD bug bounty program not later than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Chief to brief congressional defense committees on bug bounty programs within one year.

label: Segment 1
summary: Provides a framework for AI innovation and accountability through research, transparency, and risk management policies.

label: Segment 2
summary: Amends the definition of data assets to include data models and defines artificial intelligence systems.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Under Secretary of Commerce for Standards and Technology to conduct research to create and standardize methods for verifying and tracking the origin of content created by both humans and AI.

label: Segment 4
summary: Amends Section 22A(b)(1) of the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act to require the addition of detecting AI outputs and anomalies and the use of safeguards to prevent misuse.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the the Comptroller General of the United States to review and identify best practices for AI use in Federal Government.

label: Segment 6
summary: Establishes accountability measures for artificial intelligence systems.

label: Segment 7
summary: Provides relevant definitions within the instrument.

label: Segment 8
summary: Makes it unlawful to operate a covered internet platform using generative AI unless users are notified of AI-generated content.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires deployers of high-impact artificial intelligence systems to submit a report on design and safety plans for high-impact AI before deployment and annually thereafter.

label: Segment 10
summary: Makes an amendment to the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act requiring the Director to develop and regularly update sector-specific recommendations for federal agencies to oversee high-impact AI systems.

label: Segment 11
summary: Makes an amendment that mandates that within one year of enactment, the Under Secretary must submit NIST recommendations to relevant parties, who must then provide formal responses and timetables for adoption or reasons for refusal, with all documents made public.

label: Segment 12
summary: Mandates that critical-impact AI organizations conduct and report risk management assessments before public release and biennially thereafter, addressing various risk categories, with developers required to provide necessary information for compliance; the Secretary may request additional details but cannot prohibit system availability based on these reports.

label: Segment 13
summary: Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Certification Advisory Committee to advise on testing and certification standards for critical-impact AI systems, mandates the Secretary to create and periodically update a certification plan, and requires the issuance of TEVV standards, with exemptions and self-certification provisions;

label: Segment 14
summary: Authorizes the Secretary to impose civil penalties for noncompliance with AI regulations, including fines up to $300,000 or twice the transaction value, and to prohibit deployment for intentional violations; it also permits civil actions by the Attorney General for enforcement and protects sensitive information from disclosure.

label: Segment 15
summary: Mandates the creation of a working group within 180 days to recommend educational programs for artificial intelligence, focusing on informing consumers and stakeholders about AI systems.

label: Segment 1
summary: Enacts a law by the United States Congress.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the "Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act" or "AI Accountability Act".

label: Segment 3
summary: Mandates a study on accountability measures for artificial intelligence systems.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary to study AI accountability measures in communications, digital inclusion, risks, and trustworthiness.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires holding public meetings to consult stakeholders for feedback on AI accountability measures.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary to report study results, feedback, and recommendations to relevant congressional committees.

label: Segment 7
summary: Defines "accountability measure" as mechanisms ensuring system trustworthiness, including audits, assessments, or certifications.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary to hold meetings and submit a report on AI system information availability.

label: Segment 1
summary: Names the Act as the “Federal Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires agencies to incorporate NIST's AI risk management framework and guidelines into their AI efforts.

label: Segment 3
summary: Provides draft contract language requiring AI suppliers to adhere to framework and provide data access for evaluation.

label: Segment 4
summary: Mandates regulations for AI acquisition requirements and compliance, including solicitation provisions and contract clauses.

label: Segment 5
summary: Mandates the establishment of an AI expertise initiative for federal agencies within 180 days.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director of NIST to study, develop, and update AI testing and evaluation standards.

label: Segment 2
summary: Waives immunity for generative AI claims under section 230 of the Communications Act of 1934.

label: Segment 3
summary: Introduces a bill to waive Section 230 immunity for generative AI-related claims and charges.

label: Segment 4
summary: Amends Section 230 to remove immunity for claims related to generative artificial intelligence conduct.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires reports on AI regulation, establishes bug bounty programs, and mandates vulnerability analysis and data sharing.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires reports on AI regulation in finance, AI bug bounties, and AI military vulnerabilities.

label: Segment 4
summary: Cites the Act as the "Artificial Intelligence Advancement Act of 2023." Defines key terms including "congressional defense committees."

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the National Credit Union Administration, and the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection to submit a report to Congress on its gaps in knowledge relating to artificial intelligence in six key issue areas.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Department of Defense to develop a bug bounty program for foundational artificial intelligence models being integrated into Department of Defense missions and operations.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer (CDAO) of the Department of Defense to complete a study analyzing the vulnerabilities to the privacy, security, and accuracy of artificial intelligence-enabled military applications.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit to a report on ways to improve data sharing, interoperability, and quality, across the Department of Defense.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “Artificial Intelligence Environmental Impacts Act of 2024”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Finds rapid AI computational power increase and potential energy consumption rise despite efficiency gains.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines terms like artificial intelligence, AI model, AI system, and voluntary reporting entity.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the EPA to study and report AI's full lifecycle environmental impacts, including energy and pollution.

label: Segment 5
summary: Convenes a consortium to measure, report, and mitigate environmental impacts of artificial intelligence.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director to develop a voluntary AI environmental impact reporting system with public guidelines and accessibility.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires joint submission of findings, reporting system descriptions, and recommendations for AI's environmental impact to Congress.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “CLOUD AI Act”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Finds adversarial countries undermine global stability and U.S. security. Highlights China's military-civil fusion and AI weaponization.

label: Segment 3
summary:
Directs the Secretary of Commerce to prohibit United States persons and subsidiaries from providing support for the remote or cloud use of any integrated circuit listed under Export Control Classification Number 3A090 and 4A090 of the Export Administration Regulations by an entity located in the People’s Republic of China or Macau.

Allows the Secretary of Commerce to issue such regulations as necessary for this purpose.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes the authority of the Chief Digital and AI Officer Governing Council for the Department of Defense data management.

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer Governing Council, to oversee ethical AI use in the Department of Defense, consisting of senior officials and led by the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer.

label: Segment 5
summary: Outlines the duties of the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer, including streamlining AI development and oversight, improved governance coordination, and establishing ethical AI guidance and monitoring.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish a working group to coordinate an AI initiative among Five Eyes countries.

label: Segment 4
summary: Designates a senior civilian officer of the Department of Defense to head the Working Group and encourages Five Eyes member participation.

label: Segment 5
summary: Outlines responsibilities of the Working Group to develop and coordinates efforts to implement an AI initiative among Five Eyes intelligence and defense entities.

label: Segment 6
summary: Ensures knowledge and technical data from a Five Eyes country's cooperative project remains under that country's export control laws.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires submission of a plan detailing the Working Group's structure, funding, integration with defense initiatives, and performance metrics, followed by biannual reports assessing its activities and progress.

label: Segment 8
summary: Terminates the Working Group on September 30, 2028, unless extended by the Secretary of Defense.

label: Segment 9
summary: Defines terms such as "covered operational use" and "Five Eyes countries" for the purposes of the Act.

label: Segment 5
summary: Amends the Digital Equity Act to include artificial intelligence literacy in digital literacy skills.

label: Segment 6
summary: Expands the Digital Equity Competitive Grant Program to fund initiatives that promote artificial intelligence literacy in public schools and community colleges, including teacher training, curriculum development, lab equipment, and virtual learning platforms.

label: Segment 7
summary: Authorizes grants to support AI literacy by funding labs, virtual platforms, and teaching tools in higher education and by providing training and community programming through community institutions.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary to submit an annual report to Congress identifying specific areas for expansion or modification.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary to develop a public health strategy for AI threats.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to strategize public health responses to AI threats.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as “Strategy for Public Health Preparedness and Response to Artificial Intelligence Threats”.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines the term "Artificial Intelligence" by referencing the meaning assigned to the term in section 238(g) of the 2019 John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act; i.e., any artificial system—implemented in software, hardware, or other forms—that can perform human-like or rational tasks, learn from data, operate with limited human oversight, and use techniques such as machine learning to approximate cognitive functions.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary to develop and submit to Congress a strategy for AI misuse threats to national health security within 180 days, including a framework for public health preparedness and response to mitigate the risks of misuse of artificial intelligence; an outline of the duties, functions, and preparedness goals for which the Secretary is responsible; gaps in public health capabilities and strategies to address these gaps and strengthen emergency preparedness against AI threats; strategies to mitigate risks, such as the development of biological weapons and treatment-resistant viruses and bacteria.

label: Segment 6
summary: Amends the Public Health Service Act to address AI misuse threats in national health security.

label: Segment 1
summary: Directs the NIH Director to establish a grant program for generative AI research in health care.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the NIH Director to establish a grant program for generative AI research in healthcare.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as the "HEALTH AI Act."

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes grants for research on using generative AI to enhance various health care processes and outcomes.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines artificial intelligence, eligible entities, generative AI, and medically underserved population with specific legal references.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs NIST and State Department to promote U.S. leadership in AI and technology standards.

label: Segment 3
summary: Introduces a bill directing NIST and State Department to promote U.S. leadership in AI standards development.

label: Segment 4
summary: Cites the Act as the “Promoting United States Leadership in Standards Act of 2024”.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines "artificial intelligence" based on National Science and Technology Council's list. Defines "Director" as NIST's Director.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director to brief Congress on federal participation in AI and emerging technology standards development.

label: Segment 7
summary: Establishes a web portal to inform and assist U.S. industry and agencies in international AI standards efforts.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Director to establish a pilot program supporting AI standards meetings within 180 days.

label: Segment 9
summary: Defines eligible entities as organizations developing AI standards or those hosting such organizations.

label: Segment 10
summary: Awards grants to eligible entities for hosting AI standards meetings, covering up to 50% of costs.

label: Segment 11
summary: Requires the Director to develop and update guidance covering eligibility, awards, duration, merit review, and reporting.

label: Segment 12
summary: Requires the Director to brief Congress annually on the pilot program's effectiveness, grant recipients, and expenses.

label: Segment 13
summary: Requires the Director to recommend permanent implementation and submit those recommendations to Congress within three years.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the "Eliminating Bias in Algorithmic Systems Act of 2023".

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines "agency" and "covered agency" for the purposes of this Act.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines "covered algorithm" for the purposes of this Act.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires each agency to have a civil rights office addressing algorithmic bias and discrimination.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires civil rights offices to report algorithmic bias, discrimination, and mitigation steps to relevant congressional committees.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “Preventing Algorithmic Collusion Act of 2024”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines antitrust laws, commercial terms, Commission, distribution, nonpublic competitor data, nonpublic data, person, price, pricing algorithm.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires submission of detailed pricing algorithm reports within 30 days upon request by the Attorney General or Commission.

label: Segment 4
summary: Allows report sharing between DOJ, Commission, and NIST. Prohibits NIST from disclosing report contents to others.

label: Segment 5
summary: Prohibits using pricing algorithms with nonpublic competitor data.

label: Segment 6
summary: Presumes an agreement if a pricing algorithm is used to fix prices in a related market.

label: Segment 7
summary: Specifies the rebuttal conditions for defendants not developing the pricing algorithm and lacking actual knowledge.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires businesses to disclose algorithmic pricing to customers and workers before transactions.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires disclosure of price discrimination and third-party algorithms, including developer identity, if applicable.

label: Segment 10
summary: Constitutes an unfair practice under FTC Act. Allows FTC to bring civil actions and impose penalties.

label: Segment 11
summary: Mandates the FTC to study and publish findings on pricing algorithms' use, effects, and regulatory recommendations.

label: Segment 1
summary: Prohibits trade secrets privileges blocking defense evidence access in criminal cases. Establishes forensic algorithm testing standards.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires NIST to establish computational forensic algorithm testing standards within one year, addressing disparate impact assessment.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires testing software under specific conditions with various data, methodologies, and detailed performance statistics reported.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires developers to document software purpose, function, development, and testing methodology.

label: Segment 5
summary: Issues testing standards via rulemaking under section 553. Consults experts in multiple fields. Removes trade secret privilege in criminal proceedings.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires federal use of forensic algorithms to comply with NIST testing and publicize validation results.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires retesting after material software changes. Provides defendants access to forensic software and detailed analysis reports.

label: Segment 8
summary: Allows evidence if software passes NIST testing and developers waive legal claims for defense analysis.

label: Segment 9
summary: Defines "computational forensic software," "material change," and "nonmaterial change" for forensic software processes and updates.

label: Segment 1
summary: Directs the FTC to require impact assessments for automated and augmented critical decision processes.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission to mandate impact assessments for automated decision systems.

label: Segment 3
summary: Names the Act as the “Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines "augmented critical decision process" as using automated systems for critical decisions, impacting consumers substantially.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to issue regulations outlining impact-assessment guidelines for covered entities, mandating impact assessments, documentation, and annual summary reports for automated decision systems, and directing covered entities to disclose their status, consult stakeholders, mitigate negative consumer impacts, and follow standardized reporting and privacy guidelines established by the FTC.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires covered entities to conduct and document comprehensive impact assessments of automated decision systems that compare prior processes and evaluate privacy, security, performance, fairness, and consumer rights, while also mandating stakeholder consultation, data documentation, harm mitigation, staff training, and transparency measures, with flexibility in implementation and limited disclosure to summary reports.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires covered entities to submit detailed summary reports to the Federal Trade Commission containing results from impact assessments, performance testing, stakeholder consultation, data practices, transparency measures, and any additional consumer protection criteria set by the Commission.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires covered entities to disclose information regarding automated decision systems for the FTC to publish annual reports and maintain a readable repository of impact-assessment data to inform consumers, support research, and ensure transparency.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires the FTC to publish and update guidance and training materials for compliance with impact-assessment requirements, and periodically review regulations with assistance from NIST and OSTP.

label: Segment 10
summary: Establishes Bureau of Technology within the FTC for advice, enforcement, assistance; authorizes personnel appointments, inter-federal cooperation, and appropriations.

label: Segment 11
summary: Treats violations as unfair practices under FTC authority; empowers FTC, state attorneys general, and other state officials to enforce using civil jurisdiction; coordinates state and federal civil enforcement.

label: Segment 12
summary: Requires coordination with Federal and State agencies; prohibits preemption of local laws.

label: Segment 1
summary: Establishes the Joint Autonomy Office to coordinate autonomous systems delivery in the Department of Defense.

label: Segment 2
summary: Utilizes best practices to accelerate autonomous systems delivery to operational users.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes up to 12 Highly Qualified Expert positions for autonomous systems program management.

label: Segment 4
summary: Ensures the Joint Autonomy Office accesses all datasets and centralizes access, implementing necessary security protocols.

label: Segment 5
summary: Allows the Director to contract with private entities and ensures program compliance with section 3453 of title 10.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director to coordinate activities with Department of Defense policies and research programs.

label: Segment 7
summary: Terminates the Office seven years post-enactment, unless extended by the Secretary with proper notification.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines "all-domain" as multi-domain interoperability for autonomous systems. Allocates $55,000,000 for 2024.

label: Segment 9
summary: Establishes a Department-wide classification framework for autonomous capabilities within 180 days of the Joint Autonomy Office's establishment.

label: Segment 10
summary: Requires the framework to classify human supervision levels, apply to various domains, and include cost estimates.

label: Segment 11
summary: Requires the Director to report the framework details to congressional defense committees within 30 days.

label: Segment 12
summary: Mandates biennial reassessment of framework and requires briefing congressional defense committees within 30 days.

label: Segment 13
summary: Mandates the Under Secretary to instruct military services within 90 days to implement the framework.

label: Segment 14
summary: Requires the Director to develop and implement integration plans for autonomous capabilities within one year.

label: Segment 15
summary: Requires a Department-wide assessment of autonomy software integration into current and future systems.

label: Segment 16
summary: Requires an assessment of gaps in funding, acquisition processes, training, testing, and scaling commercial technologies.

label: Segment 17
summary: Requires addressing assessed gaps with updated planning for autonomy software costs and detailed future cost projections.

label: Segment 18
summary: Standardizes autonomy software acquisition across military services, consulting various Defense Department officials and organizations.

label: Segment 19
summary: Requires the Director to submit an unclassified report to congressional defense committees within 90 days.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to provide a briefing to Congress by March 30, 2024 on the national security threats posed by Chinese autonomous ground vehicles operating in the U.S., particularly near sensitive government and military sites and their potential to share data with the Chinese Communist Party.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the briefing to include the type of data that can be collected, the dual-use implications of autonomous ground vehicle technologies and their enabling factors, and how the CCP or People’s Liberation Army could potentially use the data it collects in the United States to support its military operational planning.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to carry out a pilot program to outsource basic allowance for housing rate calculation using AI.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary to submit a report evaluating rates calculated through the pilot program to Armed Services Committees.

label: Segment 1
summary: Titles the Act as the “Healthy Technology Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines AI as a licensed practitioner if state-authorized and federally approved to prescribe drugs.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “MARTTE Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines key terms used in the Act, including computer programming and AI-related occupational areas, digital personnel platforms, and human capital infrastructure for developing and tracking technical skills.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines the qualification process for military computer programming and AI-related roles, including eligible training, experiences, and education, and defines the standard used to assess whether personnel meet required capabilities.

label: Segment 4
summary: Finds the need to build infrastructure for military computer programming skills and workforce development.

label: Segment 5
summary: Establishes a policy for the Armed Forces to build a robust human capital infrastructure for computer programming, coding, and AI-related skills.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the development of occupational codes and special skills designators aligned with computer programming, AI, machine learning, and software engineering roles to support identifying and managing personnel with these capabilities.

label: Segment 7
summary: Conducts assessments and maintains awareness of Armed Forces members who are qualified or skilled in computer programming and related areas.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires military departments to submit implementation plans for the policy and provide ongoing updates in their budget justification materials.

label: Segment 9
summary: Finds the Department of Defense lacks mechanisms to leverage reserve components' civilian computer programming skills.

label: Segment 10
summary: Establishes policy to leverage reserve members’ civilian-acquired computer programming and related skills by evaluating, tracking, and using them to meet military needs.

label: Segment 11
summary: Assigns responsibility to the Secretary of Defense in collaboration with several other officials for policy development and discharge to designated officers or employees.

label: Segment 12
summary: Requires the Secretary to establish qualification processes, assess skills, develop tracking platforms, and integrate skilled members.

label: Segment 13
summary: Mandates submission of implementation plans and ongoing budget updates by each military department and the National Guard Bureau.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “Artificial Intelligence Leadership Training Act” or “AI Leadership Training Act”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines key terms for AI leadership training program, including "AI," "covered employee," "Director," and "executive agency."

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes an AI leadership training program for employees, incorporating relevant existing training, within 18 months.

label: Segment 4
summary: Ensures covered employees know AI capabilities, risks, safety, ethics, federal requirements, and best practices.

label: Segment 5
summary: Includes information on AI fundamentals, risks, benefits, data's role, mitigation strategies, organizational considerations, and constitutional risks.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director to update the Program biennially to incorporate new AI information and meet requirements.

label: Segment 7
summary: Establishes means to measure participation and receive feedback to improve the Program.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Director to provide requested Program information to Congress within 14 days.

label: Segment 9
summary: Ensures the Director includes expert interactions in training. Terminates this section 10 years post-enactment.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes a task force on artificial intelligence in the financial services sector.

label: Segment 4
summary: Outlines the required composition of the task force, which must include the Secretary of the Treasury, Comptroller of the Currency, Chairman of the Federal Reserve, Chairperson of the FDIC, the Director of the CFPB, the Chairman of the NCUA, and the Director of the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network, or designees.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the task force to produce a report for Congress that contains public feedback from financial services industry stakeholders.

label: Segment 6
summary:
Requires the aforementioned task force report to detail how financial institutions protect themselves from AI fraud, potential risks associated with the use of artificial intelligence by bad actors and legislative recommendations for AI regulations to protect consumers from data theft and fraud.

Terminates the task force 90 days after the final report is submitted to Congress.

label: Segment 1
summary: Directs the DOE and NOAA to advance numerical weather and climate prediction through collaborative research.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “Advanced Weather Model Computing Development Act”. Defines key terms including Department and Secretary.

label: Segment 3
summary: Mandates collaborative AI research for climate models and weather prediction between the Department of Energy and the NOAA.

label: Segment 4
summary: Mandates the Administrator to run advanced weather models using high performance computers for comparative research, through a competitive process including other agencies and organizations.

label: Segment 5
summary: Applies the activities consistent with subtitle D of title VI of the Research and Development Act.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission to establish standards for making publicly available information about the training data and algorithms used in AI foundation models.

label: Segment 3
summary: Finds increased AI access leads to more copyright lawsuits and public concerns about biased information.

label: Segment 4
summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission, in consultation with relevant stakeholders, to issue and routinely update standards for the public disclosure of specific information about AI foundational model training data and algorithms within 9 months.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines relevant terms, including "artificial intelligence," "foundational model," and "training data."

label: Segment 1
summary: Establishes the Chief AI Officers Council, Chief AI Officers, and AI Governance Boards.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “AI LEAD Act” and defines key terms including "agency", "artificial intelligence", and "Council".

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes a Chief Artificial Intelligence Officers Council to promote coordination and manage AI risks in government.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires agency heads to ensure responsible AI use, appoint Chief AI Officer, and inform Congress within 1 year.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires agencies to establish AI Governance Boards and strategies for trustworthy AI adoption within 120 days. Requires a report to the GAO on effectiveness of governance boards.

label: Segment 1
summary: Provides for Department of Energy and National Science Foundation research and development coordination.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “DOE and NSF Interagency Research Act.” Requires that the Secretary of Energy and the Director of the National Science Foundation collaborate on their research and complete a memorandum of understanding.

label: Segment 3
summary: Conducts collaborative research and support in diverse focus areas including plasma science, quantum computing, and advanced manufacturing technologies.

label: Segment 4
summary: Authorizes reimbursable agreements and collaborations with Federal agencies. Requires submission of a detailed report.

label: Segment 1
summary: Introduces a bill to protect individual property rights in likeness and voice.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “No AI FRAUD Act” of 2024. Highlights AI misuse affecting voice and likeness.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines terms for likeness and voice rights, including individual, digital depiction, and personalized cloning service.

label: Segment 4
summary: Grants individuals property rights in their likeness and voice, freely transferable and descendible after death.

label: Segment 5
summary: Prohibits unauthorized use of voice or likeness in digital forms or cloning services without consent.

label: Segment 6
summary: Balances public interest against intellectual property interests in First Amendment defense for voice or likeness use.

label: Segment 7
summary: Limits liability for negligible harm from unauthorized use of voice or likeness under specific conditions.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes a task force to assess the privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties implications of AI; defines membership of task force.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the AI Task Force to assess existing regulatory gaps and recommend reforms for AI use in federal operations addressing specific considerations such as ensuring the integrity of data and the acquisition of new AI systems.

label: Segment 4
summary: Empowers the AI Task Force to hold hearings, take authorized actions, and obtain necessary official data.

label: Segment 5
summary: Outlines procedures for voting and adopting recommendations.

label: Segment 6
summary: Allows the task force to appoint staff, detail federal personnel, issue security clearances, and commission expert consultants.

label: Segment 7
summary: Allows the Chair of the task force to engage private sector representatives and temporarily assign their employees to the Task Force.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the AI Task Force to provide updates and submit interim and final reports on their assessment of organizational considerations.

label: Segment 1
summary: Prohibits email providers from flagging opted-in political campaign emails as spam using filtering algorithms.

label: Segment 2
summary: Prohibits email services from algorithmically labeling political emails without user direction. Requires quarterly transparency reports.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires email service operators to provide spam-related disclosure reports to political campaigns upon request.

label: Segment 4
summary: Treats violations as unfair or deceptive practices under FTC Act. Grants FTC enforcement powers and authority.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines "filtering algorithm," "operator," and "political campaign" with specific criteria and exclusions.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the FCC to prohibit AI features for minors without parental consent, in consultation with the FTC.

label: Segment 3
summary: Titles the Act as the “AI Shield for Kids Act” or “ASK Act” and defines the relevant terms artificial intelligence, minor, product, and user.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the FCC to issue rules on AI consent for minors within 180 days.

label: Segment 1
summary: Authorizes EPA Administrator to award grants and contracts for emerging technologies addressing water quality threats.

label: Segment 2
summary: States that the EPA should prioritize the use of emerging technologies, including artificial technology, to address issues of water quality.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes the Water Quality and Environmental Innovation Fund for grants and contracts until 2028.

label: Segment 4
summary: Allows awarding grants for projects using artificial intelligence to address threats to water quality and requires disclosure of data.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Administrator to annually report on emerging technologies' benefits, grant recipients, project goals, and effectiveness.

label: Segment 6
summary: Refers to artificial intelligence as being defined in section 5002 of the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020 (15 U.S.C. 9401).

label: Segment 1
summary: Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource to democratize AI research access and foster diversity.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes a NAIRR Steering Subcommittee chaired by the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Director to select, review, and update NAIRR Steering Subcommittee members and establish subcommittees.

label: Segment 4
summary: Coordinates with stakeholders to oversee NAIRR operations, establish criteria, evaluate performance, and provide resources.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines key terms amending the National AI Initiative Act to establish the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource.

label: Segment 6
summary: Directs the National Science Foundation to establish the National AI Research Resource within one year to promote safe, reliable, and trustworthy AI development, expand equitable research access, and support testing and evaluation of AI systems.

label: Segment 7
summary: Establishes a Program Management Office to oversee NAIRR's functions, requiring at least three full-time employees.

label: Segment 8
summary: Directs the Program Management Office to manage funding opportunities, select an Operating Entity, oversee compliance, and coordinate federal resource contributions.

label: Segment 9
summary: Permits the Operating Entity to manage NAIRR operations by maintaining access portals, developing policies, hiring staff, setting performance indicators, and publicly reporting annual performance results.

label: Segment 10
summary: Directs the Operating Entity to train users on ethical and trustworthy AI practices, link them to AI testbeds, and share NAIRR resources to educate federal personnel.

label: Segment 11
summary: Establishes Advisory Committees to advise NAIRR's leadership, comprising members from various sectors.

label: Segment 12
summary: Directs the Program Management Office to manage and review NAIRR resources, ensure ethical and privacy safeguards, and publicly report available AI research resources and their sources.

label: Segment 13
summary: Requires the NAIRR to provide computing environments, cloud services, open-source tools, and APIs to support AI research and model development.

label: Segment 14
summary: Publishes interoperability standards, creates and selects data repositories, establishes criteria, provides access, and coordinates data sharing.

label: Segment 15
summary: Facilitates educational tools and AI testbeds, provides training, support, and outreach for underrepresented STEM groups.

label: Segment 16
summary: Allows U.S.-based researchers, educators, students, and certain employees access to NAIRR.

label: Segment 17
summary: Defines eligible entities as U.S.-based institutions, agencies, nonprofits, small businesses, consortia, or designated categories.

label: Segment 18
summary: Prohibits individuals from countries listed in section 4872(d)(2) as eligible users; mandates NSF Director enforcement.

label: Segment 19
summary: Establishes requirements for the Program Management Office and auditing for privacy, ethics, civil rights, safety, and AI trustworthiness compliance.

label: Segment 20
summary: Requires prioritizing AI projects addressing privacy, ethics, civil rights, and other public interest topics.

label: Segment 21
summary: Develops guidance for scientific integrity, addressing research impacts and reporting violations, consistent with government policies.

label: Segment 22
summary: Establishes security requirements for NAIRR; permits additional controls; creates tiered fee schedule with free access.

label: Segment 23
summary: Ensures conformance with national policy on research security. Designates a research security point of contact.

label: Segment 3
summary: Expands the definition of “electioneering communication” to include paid online and digital political ads on large online platforms, applies transparency requirements to them, and clarifies that news exemptions do not apply to content from politically affiliated sources.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires political advertisements containing AI-generated images or videos to include clear disclaimers stating such content was created with AI, with specific formatting rules based on the type of media, and mandates the Federal Election Commission to issue implementing regulations.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Federal Election Commission to submit a report to Congress on compliance, recommend modifications, and identify ways to enhance transparency in political advertisements.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires generative AI to disclose AI-generated output.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires AI-generated outputs to include a disclaimer and enforces via the Federal Trade Commission with potential for fines and civil penalties.

label: Segment 1
summary: Amends the Financial Stability Act to assign AI-related duties to the Financial Stability Oversight Council.

label: Segment 2
summary: Names the Act as the “Financial Artificial Intelligence Risk Reduction Act” or the “FAIRR Act”.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Council to assess AI risks, report gaps, and recommend financial regulation improvements.

label: Segment 4
summary: Amends the Federal Credit Union Act to enhance authority over third-party AI service providers.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires regulated entities to notify the Director of service relationships within 30 days.

label: Segment 6
summary: Amends to impose treble penalties for machine-manipulated media violations. Holds AI model deployers liable under securities laws.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes an artificial intelligence commission for various purposes.

label: Segment 3
summary: Titles the Act as the "National AI Commission Act."

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes the National AI Commission to review, regulate, and recommend oversight structures for artificial intelligence.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission to require disclosures for telemarketing that uses AI and increase penalties for AI identity impersonation over telecommunication channels.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires companies to disclose their usage of AI to impersonate human beings in telemarketing calls and texts.

label: Segment 4
summary: Levels civil penalties on entities that violate this Act and doubles maximum amount of civil penalties for entities that violate cease-and-desist orders issued under this Act.

label: Segment 5
summary: Amends the Telemarketing and Consumer Fraud and Abuse Prevention Act by adding text messages into the scope of coverage.

label: Segment 2
summary: Promotes a 21st century artificial intelligence workforce through legislative measures.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as the "Jobs of the Future Act of 2023." Identifies key AI workforce impact areas.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the Secretary of Labor and NSF Director to submit interim and final AI impact reports within one year.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires identification of data, industries, opportunities, demographics, skills, methods, leaders, resources, and recommendations on AI workforce impact.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires collaboration with educational institutions, industry stakeholders, National Academies, and federal officials for report preparation.

label: Segment 7
summary: Defines terms: "artificial intelligence," "community college," "institution of higher education," "local educational agency".

label: Segment 2
summary: Amends the Communications Act to require AI robocall disclosures and enhance penalties for AI impersonation violations.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as the “QUIET Act”.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires disclosure of AI use at the start of robocalls or text messages.

label: Segment 5
summary: Imposes doubled penalties for AI-driven voice or text impersonation intended to defraud, harm, or obtain value.

label: Segment 3
summary: Prohibits the distribution of AI-generated deceptive audio impersonating a candidate's voice with the intent of misleading voters, except for specific media, news and satire exceptions.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines terms such as "materially deceptive audio" as AI-generated audio mimicking a candidate's voice without disclosure.

label: Segment 5
summary: Imposes fines or up to two years of imprisonment on those who knowingly violate the prohibition on deceptive AI-generated audio in political communications.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the FEC to issue regulations within 90 days to clarify satire exemptions and disclaimer requirements, and submit annual compliance reports.

label: Segment 1
summary: Prohibits undisclosed worker data surveillance by employers; mandates disclosure and transparency.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines "Administrator" as Privacy and Technology Division's head. Defines "aggregated data" as non-identifiable combined data. Defines "applicant" as job seeker.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines "automated decision system" as software or process using computation to influence outcomes, excluding passive infrastructure.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines biometric information as data from unique biological traits, excluding photographs and non-identifying recordings.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines "covered individual" as anyone working for or applying to an employer for remuneration.

label: Segment 6
summary: Defines “data” broadly to include any information linked to a worker or applicant, covering personal, workplace, biometric, sensor, and online information, including inputs and outputs of automated decision systems.

label: Segment 7
summary: Defines “employ” and “employer” to determine which entities and individuals are subject to the Act’s provisions, including public and private employers with 11 or more workers.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines key terms including “employment-related decision” covering hiring, firing, pay, and scheduling determinations; “precise geolocation information” covering location-tracking data; and other administrative or legal entities such as government agencies, labor organizations, and arbitration agreements.

label: Segment 9
summary: Defines key administrative and technical terms, including “service provider” for entities that process employer data, “sell” regarding data transfers, and “technologist,” explicitly including artificial intelligence and related computational fields.

label: Segment 10
summary: Defines “third party” and “transfer” in the context of employer data sharing and “workplace surveillance” as any form of data monitoring or collection about workers’ activities or communications, including through digital, sensor, or wearable technologies.

label: Segment 11
summary: Requires employers to disclose and regularly update detailed information on workplace surveillance, including data collected, collection methods, use, storage, third-party sharing, and its role in employment decisions, in a publicly accessible and machine-readable format.

label: Segment 12
summary: Requires employers to provide accessible, plain-language, risk-based disclosures; obtain acknowledgment of receipt from workers; and allow workers to access and correct data collected through workplace surveillance within seven days of request.

label: Segment 13
summary: Prohibits employers and their third-party service providers from using workplace surveillance or AI-enabled monitoring to identify or track union activity, political or religious beliefs, health or disability status, immigration status, or whistleblowing activity of workers.

label: Segment 14
summary: Prohibits employers from using automated decision systems to predict off-duty behavior, from surveilling workers in private or sensitive locations, from using surveillance in ways that harm workers’ health, from using collected data for undisclosed purposes, and from selling worker data to others.

label: Segment 15
summary: Prohibits employers and third parties from transferring personally identifiable data collected through workplace surveillance without disclosure, consent, and appropriate cybersecurity and encryption protections.

label: Segment 16
summary: Requires employers to disclose when an employment decision is made using data from workplace surveillance and to allow affected workers to review and correct that data within seven days.

label: Segment 17
summary: Limits employers to collecting only data reasonably related to business operations, restricts internal access to such data based on need, and requires contracts with third parties conducting workplace surveillance to include compliance with these restrictions.

label: Segment 18
summary: Establishes a Privacy and Technology Division within the Department of Labor, led by a presidentially appointed Administrator empowered to hire technologists and other staff to carry out the division’s duties under the Act.

label: Segment 19
summary: Requires the Administrator to establish four advisory boards composed of experts and representatives from labor, technology, research, and product fields to advise on employer data practices and emerging technologies.

label: Segment 20
summary: Establishes compensation and travel reimbursement for advisory board members, exempts the boards from the Federal Advisory Committee Act, authorizes voluntary services, and allows the Administrator’s attorneys to represent them in litigation.

label: Segment 21
summary: Establishes the headquarters of the Privacy and Technology Division in Washington, D.C., authorizes additional regional offices, and empowers the Secretary and Administrator to issue orders and guidance in coordination with other federal agencies on privacy and labor issues.

label: Segment 22
summary: Authorizes the Secretary of Labor, through the Privacy and Technology Division, to issue regulations implementing the Act in consultation with agencies such as the FTC, EEOC, and NLRB, with the GAO and Library of Congress issuing their own rules for their employees.

label: Segment 23
summary: Requires the Office of Compliance and the President to issue regulations for congressional and executive branch employees that align with those prescribed by the Secretary of Labor, allowing modifications if needed for effective implementation.

label: Segment 24
summary: Directs the Director of the Office of Personnel Management to issue regulations for federal employees covered under Title 5 consistent with those of the Secretary of Labor, with limited authority to modify them for effective implementation.

label: Segment 25
summary: Prohibits employers from retaliating against workers who report or participate in investigations of violations of this Act, including those related to workplace surveillance and data privacy.

label: Segment 26
summary: Grants the Privacy and Technology Division investigative authority to ensure compliance with this Act, including power to require employer reports, conduct joint investigations, and coordinate with other agencies on enforcement.

label: Segment 27
summary: Requires employers and service providers to maintain and provide compliance records, authorizes the Secretary of Labor to investigate and enforce violations, prioritize industries with extensive workplace surveillance, refer criminal cases to the Attorney General, and litigate through the Solicitor of Labor.

label: Segment 28
summary: Authorizes workers and labor organizations to bring civil actions in federal court for violations of workplace surveillance, automated decision, and whistleblower protections, allowing recovery of damages, attorney’s fees, and injunctive or equitable relief.

label: Segment 29
summary: Specifies statutory damages for violations of disclosure, surveillance misuse, and whistleblower protections, outlining monetary penalties and factors courts must consider in assessing damages, and requiring prior notice to the Secretary and relevant State attorney general before filing suit.

label: Segment 30
summary: Authorizes the Secretary and State attorneys general to intervene in civil actions, provides for waiver of sovereign immunity for states and tribal governments receiving federal funds, and allows suits against state or tribal officials for injunctive or equitable relief.

label: Segment 31
summary: Delegates enforcement authority for this Act to the Comptroller General and Librarian of Congress for their employees, and extends equivalent powers and remedies under existing federal employment laws to employees covered by the Congressional Accountability Act, Title 3, and Title 5 of the U.S. Code.

label: Segment 32
summary: Authorizes state attorneys general and privacy regulators to enforce the Act through civil actions, makes employers liable for violations by third-party surveillance providers they use, and prohibits mandatory arbitration or class-action waivers for claims under this Act.

label: Segment 33
summary: Requires the Secretary of Labor to study and report annually to Congress on technological workplace surveillance, coordinate with other agencies for consistent regulation, and clarifies that the Act does not preempt other laws while remaining severable if any provision is found unconstitutional.

label: Segment 4
summary: Outlines the certification of AI software in the production of agricultural products based on NIST standards.

label: Segment 1
summary: Improves SBA programs to assist small businesses in accessing broadband technology.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites this Act as the “Small Business Broadband and Emerging Information Technology Enhancement Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 3
summary: Amends the Small Business Act to establish a broadband and emerging information technology coordinator position.

label: Segment 4
summary: Designates a senior employee as broadband coordinator with specific responsibilities that develop the use of emerging technologies for small businesses, as well as limited international travel.

label: Segment 5
summary: Provides training to Administration employees on broadband and emerging information technologies and related federal assistance programs.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires biennial reports on broadband activities and a study on broadband's impact on small businesses.

label: Segment 7
summary: Amends the Small Business Act to enhance entrepreneurial access to broadband and emerging information technologies.

label: Segment 2
summary: Improves the requirement for establishing AI testbeds and enhances interagency coordination for trustworthy AI systems.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as the "TEST AI Act of 2023".

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the Director to establish AI testbeds with interagency and private sector coordination for trustworthy AI development.

label: Segment 1
summary: Introduces a bill to enhance precision agriculture participation in the United States.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “Promoting Precision Agriculture Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines key terms: advanced wireless tech, AI, foreign adversary, precision agriculture, trusted, voluntary consensus standards.

label: Segment 4
summary: Enhances participation in precision agriculture and promotes leadership in setting relevant U.S. standards.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires developing interconnectivity standards for precision agriculture, consulting multiple stakeholders, and considering AI impacts.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Comptroller General to assess and report on precision agriculture standards every two years.

label: Segment 1
summary: Expands AI training within the executive branch of the federal government.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “AI Training Expansion Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes guidelines for what the AI training program should cover, including what AI is and how it works, potential benefits and risks associated with AI, and what executive agencies should consider in deploying and managing AI.

label: Segment 1
summary: Facilitates joint research by the Department of Energy and Department of Agriculture for specified purposes.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the "DOE and USDA Interagency Research Act".

label: Segment 3
summary: Mandates joint R&D activities with a memorandum requiring competitive, merit-reviewed processes for project approval.

label: Segment 4
summary: Allows collaborative research in AI, fosters data integration, promotes partnerships, and supports efficient, eco-friendly agriculture.

label: Segment 5
summary: Authorize reimbursable agreements, require interagency report submission, apply research security measures.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes the Supply Chain Resiliency and Crisis Response Office in the Department of Commerce.

label: Segment 3
summary: Cites the Act as the “Building Resilient Supply Chains Act”.

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes an office to enhance U.S. critical supply chains' resilience, security, and economic competitiveness.

label: Segment 5
summary: Supports supply chain resilience through grants, loans, and guarantees for eligible activities by eligible entities.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Under Secretary to determine financial necessity and practical assistance before awarding grants, loans, or guarantees.

label: Segment 7
summary: Limits federal grant cost share to 80%, allows waivers during emergencies, and requires creditworthiness for loans.

label: Segment 8
summary: Authorizes the Under Secretary to prescribe rates, regulate forms, develop metrics, and revoke financial assistance.

label: Segment 9
summary: Establishes the "Supply Chains for Critical Manufacturing Industries Fund" for critical supply chain resilience.

label: Segment 10
summary: Authorizes $41 billion for loans, grants, and administrative costs; $5 million for Commerce Inspector General. Ensures international agreement consistency and workforce protections.

label: Segment 11
summary: Defines terms related to national security, critical industries, key technology areas, domestic enterprises, and supply chains.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines algorithmic processes as including any artificial intelligence technique as used in social media, automated decision making, the generation of content, and more.

label: Segment 6
summary: Establishes the Federal Digital Platform Commission to regulate digital platforms including that algorithmic processes used are fair, transparent, and safe.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires recommendation systems for algorithmic processes to ensure fairness and transparency with regular public risk assessments.

label: Segment 11
summary: Establishes the Code Council to develop behavioral codes, technical standards, and policies for digital platforms using algorithmic processes.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires social media platforms to verify users' age, prohibit under-18 recommendations, and block under-13 access.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines key terms for the “Protecting Kids on Social Media Act” including algorithmic systems, minors, and social media platforms.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires social media platforms to take reasonable steps for age verification beyond mere attestation.

label: Segment 4
summary: Prohibits children under 13 from using social media platforms, except for passive content viewing.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires social media platforms to obtain verified parental consent for minor users' accounts.

label: Segment 6
summary: Prohibits the use of algorithmic recommendation systems on individuals under 18.

label: Segment 7
summary: Establishes a secure digital identification pilot program for U.S. citizens and residents, ensuring privacy and cybersecurity.

label: Segment 8
summary: Treats violations by social media platforms as unfair or deceptive acts or practices.

label: Segment 9
summary: Allows state attorneys general to sue social media platforms violating the Act for civil penalties, and outlines the powers of the Federal Trade Commission with respect to these matters.

label: Segment 1
summary: Titles the Act as the “Supply Chain Mapping and Monitoring Act”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines terms for critical goods, industries, infrastructure, domestic enterprises, domestic manufacturers, economically distressed areas, agencies, and industrial equipment.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines the key technology focus areas, including AI, quantum science, robotics, biotechnology, data storage, advance energy, and advanced materials.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines "labor organization" to include labor unions, federations, and organizations representing various public and private employees.

label: Segment 5
summary: Establishes an office to enhance U.S. leadership in supply chains and promote resilience and crisis response.

label: Segment 6
summary: Monitors supply chains' resilience and strength. Supports critical industries' development. Minimizes supply chain shocks' effects.

label: Segment 7
summary: Appoints the Under Secretary for a 5-year term, requiring Presidential nomination and Senate approval.

label: Segment 8
summary: Establishes a unified coordination group to acquire, study, and share supply chain information, and develop contingency plans.

label: Segment 9
summary: Authorizes international agreements to enhance supply chain security. Requires designations, public comments, and periodic updates.

label: Segment 10
summary: Requires the Secretary to report annually to Congress and coordinate with federal agencies on implementation.

label: Segment 11
summary: Exempts voluntarily submitted supply chain information from disclosure and unauthorized use, except for semiconductor incentive applications.

label: Segment 12
summary: Requires the Secretary to report on implementation and coordinate with relevant offices within one year.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “AI for National Security Act”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Amends Section 1521 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 to expand the Department of Defense's procurement of cyber data products and services to include AI-driven endpoint security and to strengthen software supply chain security.

label: Segment 1
summary: Authorizes the Secretary of Commerce to review and prohibit transactions with foreign adversaries.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the "RESTRICT Act".

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines key terms used throughout the Act, including classified information, controlling and covered holdings, covered transactions, ICTS entities, foreign adversaries, and critical infrastructure.

label: Segment 4
summary: Authorizes the Secretary to identify, prohibit, or mitigate ICT transactions or services that pose unacceptable national security, critical infrastructure, or election-related risks.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary to identify ICT holdings that pose undue or unacceptable national security risks and refer them to the President for potential divestment or mitigation.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Secretary to prioritize evaluation of high-risk ICT products and services in covered transactions, including telecommunications systems, data-hosting platforms, large consumer apps, AI and machine learning systems, autonomous and robotic technologies, quantum technologies, and other critical capabilities.

label: Segment 7
summary: Allows the Secretary, in consultation with the Director of National Intelligence, to designate or remove foreign adversaries and requires classified notice to congressional leadership 15 days in advance.

label: Segment 8
summary: Establishes an expedited congressional process to disapprove the Secretary of Commerce’s designation or removal of a foreign adversary.

label: Segment 9
summary: Empowers the Secretary to establish regulations, review covered transactions, issue guidance, create lists of foreign persons, and convene technical advisory committees to implement this Act.

label: Segment 10
summary: Requires parties to provide complete information under oath for transactions under review and authorizes the Secretary to compel documents, testimony, and disclosures subject to national security confidentiality rules.

label: Segment 11
summary: Authorizes the President, Secretary of Commerce, and federal agencies to investigate and enforce violations of the Act, including through inspections, seizures, subpoenas, and court actions.

label: Segment 12
summary: Prohibits violations of regulations issued under the Act and establishes civil and criminal penalties, including fines, imprisonment, and property forfeiture.

label: Segment 13
summary: Defines “classified information” and restricts administrative and judicial review of actions under the Act, allowing only limited appeals to the D.C. Circuit with strict procedures for handling sensitive information.

label: Segment 14
summary: Clarifies that this Act does not alter existing Federal authorities, including CFIUS and other national-security review powers, and limits when the Secretary may review certain ICT transactions.

label: Segment 15
summary: Continues all administrative actions taken under Executive Order 13873 and the International Emergency Economic Powers Act until modified or revoked under this Act.

label: Segment 16
summary: Exempts the Act’s implementation from the Paperwork Reduction Act and FOIA, authorizes direct hiring to expedite execution, and provides for necessary appropriations.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the Secretary of Energy and NOAA Administrator to collaborate on advancing U.S. weather models.

label: Segment 3
summary: Titles the Act as the "Advanced Weather Model Computing Development Act".

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines "advanced computing techniques" as necessary hardware, software, and computational methods—such as artificial intelligence and high-performance computing—required to develop and deploy an "advanced weather model," which is a model used to predict Earth system processes across various time scales.

label: Segment 5
summary: Mandates the Secretary and Administrator to carry out collaborative research and development activities using advanced computing techniques to improve advanced weather models through agreements with institutions of higher education, nonprofit institutions, and other entities; requires the Secretary and the Administrator to report to Congress on interagency coordination, technical capabilities, research achievements, future gains, and the continuation of collaborative activities within two years of the Act's enactment.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Secretary and Administrator to carry out an initiative to develop and deploy advanced weather models using advanced computing techniques through a competitive, merit-reviewed process; permits establishment of up to three centers of excellence at National Laboratories to research, develop, and deploy advanced computing techniques for weather models; requires the Administrator to submit a report to Congress evaluating the Initiative's effectiveness, opportunities to expand advanced computing capabilities, and the need for interagency collaboration, with the authority for this entire section terminating five years after enactment.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires consistency with subtitle D of title VI of the Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act.

label: Segment 1
summary: Expands NIST functions to include AI workforce framework and updates cybersecurity education framework.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “Technology Workforce Framework Act of 2024”.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines competencies, workforce categories, and workforce framework per the NIST Act amendment.

label: Segment 4
summary: Expands the Director's functions to develop and update workforce frameworks for critical and emerging technologies.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Director to report on updates and applications of the NICE Workforce Framework for Cybersecurity.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Director to assess, develop, and update workforce frameworks for emerging technologies, including AI.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines "automation" as technology functioning without continuous operator input, including autonomous vehicle technology or autonomous machinery technology.

label: Segment 5
summary: Authorizes grants for training workers affected by automation, requiring competitive applications and lasting up to 4 years.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires each application to describe project participants, training services, goals, technology-based skills, and support plans.

label: Segment 7
summary: Prioritizes grants for partnerships in high-need areas, automation-impacted industries, and those offering worker training benefits.

label: Segment 8
summary: Directs that grants shall be used for workforce training, employer support, equipment, job search assistance, stipends, and integrated education.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires eligible partnerships to report training outcomes, earnings, best practices, and demographics within a year of completing the demonstration or pilot project.

label: Segment 10
summary: Requires grant use to align with labor standards and nondiscrimination and authorizes funding for worker training expansion.

label: Segment 1
summary: Cites the Act as the “FinCEN Modernization Act of 2023”.

label: Segment 2
summary: Modernizes the research, development, information sharing, and acquisition process of the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires FinCEN to establish programs for technological advances, information sharing, and monitoring financial systems.

label: Segment 4
summary: Authorizes FinCEN to perform transactions with various entities under specific terms for paragraph (1) requirements.

label: Segment 5
summary: Authorizes FinCEN to accept contributions, gifts, or services deemed appropriate by the Director for five years.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires FinCEN to annually report transactions, justifications, and expenditures to Senate and House committees.

label: Segment 1

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites this Act as the "Preventing PLA Acquisition of United States Technology Act of 2023".

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines Chinese entities of concern and covered entities related to military-civil fusion strategy limitations.

label: Segment 4
summary: Prohibits engaging or partnering with Chinese entities in strategic military-civil fusion technology research or exchange.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish and update a website listing prohibited research areas and Chinese entities.

label: Segment 6
summary: Exempts collaborative research not advancing military-civil fusion, as determined by Defense regulations.

label: Segment 7
summary: Mandates annual reports on Chinese research ties to the Secretary of Defense by covered entities.

label: Segment 8
summary: Precludes violators from receiving federal aid and mandates regulation and coordination for enforcing prohibitions.

label: Segment 1

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as the “Manufacturing Economy And National Security Act” or “MEANS Act”.

label: Segment 4

summary: Appoints the Under Secretary for a 5-year term with Senate consent, compensated at Executive Schedule level II.

summary: Supports supply chain resilience by providing loans and guarantees to eligible entities with detailed applications.

summary: Allows the development, support, and expansion of supply chains, manufacturing, and critical infrastructure in the United States.

summary: Defines terms including "account," "admission," "appropriate congressional committees," "financial institution," "foreign person," and "military invasion."

label: Segment 11
summary: Prohibits trading of Chinese-affiliated entities' securities on U.S. exchanges after specific determinations.

label: Segment 12

label: Segment 13
summary: Prohibits importing Chinese goods unless deemed necessary for U.S. security, with presidential and congressional approval.

label: Segment 14
summary: Exempts intelligence activities and permits presidential waiver for national security interests with congressional notification.

summary: Authorizes the President to implement, establishing penalties for violations in accordance with the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

label: Segment 1
summary: Directs the Secretary of Education to provide grants for increasing access to data literacy education.

label: Segment 2
summary: Cites the Act as "Data Science and Literacy Act of 2023"; lists contents and grant program details.

label: Segment 3
summary: Finds data science and literacy vital for U.S. residents' competitiveness, well-being, and workforce development.

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes a grant program for eligible entities to expand data literacy education, improve student data reasoning skills, and develop model programs.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires eligible entities to apply for grants by outlining how they will expand data literacy and STEM education, evaluate project activities, and continue them after the grant period.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires eligible entities to use grant funds for at least two approved data science education activities, with additional options for certain institutions and a 15 percent limit on equipment spending.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires recipients to submit biannual reports on grant fund usage and student demographics, and directs the Secretary to publish a five-year report and evaluation of the program’s effectiveness.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines key terms used in the grant program, including data literacy, data science, data science education, and eligible entities.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes a program within NOAA to improve precipitation forecasts.

label: Segment 3

label: Segment 4
summary: Mandates NOAA Administrator to update program goals biennially. Allocates specific appropriations through fiscal year 2028.

label: Segment 2
summary: Authorizes the Secretary of Energy and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration to carry out collaborative research and development activities through a Memoranda of Understanding, or other appropriate agreements.

summary: Proposes collaborative activities to include research of focus areas, including machine learning, and the development of data sets, data sharing, and shared infrastructure access.

label: Segment 5
