summary: Develop new export controls on semiconductor sub-systems to enhance national security and prevent adversarial misuse.

label: Segment 36
summary: Imposes strong export controls and aligns measures with allies to prevent sensitive technology backfilling.

label: Segment 37
summary: Ensures U.S. Government evaluates frontier AI systems for national security risks and vulnerabilities with experts' collaboration.

label: Segment 38
summary: Requires federally funded institutions to use nucleic acid synthesis tools with robust screening. Establish enforcement mechanisms.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines terms for the document, including artificial intelligence model, catastrophic risk, and critical safety incident.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines ai-related terms, including deploy, foundation model, frontier AI framework, and large frontier developer.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires large frontier developers to write, comply with, and conspicuously publish a frontier AI framework that includes an assessment of catastrophic risks, incorporates national and international standards, and creates mitigation strategies.

label: Segment 6
summary: Creates reporting requirements for large frontier developers, including yearly updates to frontier AI frameworks and transparency reports for new or modified frontier models, which include assessments of catastrophic risks.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires large frontier developers to submit a quarterly summary of catastrophic risk assessments to the Office of Emergency Services; prohibits large frontier developers from making false statements about catastrophic risk or compliance with frontier AI frameworks; permits redactions in published documents to protect trade secrets, cybersecurity, or national security.

label: Segment 8
summary: Creates reporting requirements, including that the Office of Emergency Services establish a reporting mechanism for critical safety incidents used by developers and the public, and a mechanism for frontier developers to confidentially submit catastrophic risk assessment summaries.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires the Office of Emergency Services to produce an annual report with anonymized information about critical safety incidents; permits the Office of Emergency Services to designate federal regulations, laws, or guidance documents that frontier developers can invoke to comply with the requirements of subdivision H.

label: Segment 10
summary: Requires the Department of Technology to make recommendations about whether and how to update the definitions of AI-related terms, including frontier model and frontier developer, and submit those recommendations to the Legislature; instructs the Attorney General to submit a report to the Legislature with anonymized information about reports pursuant to chapter 5.1.

label: Segment 11
summary: Creates a civil penalty of up to $1,000,000 for large frontier developers who fail to comply with the requirements of this chapter.

label: Segment 12
summary: Establishes a consortium within the Government Operations Agency to develop a framework for Calcompute, a public cloud computing cluster to advance the development and deployment of AI; lists what Calcompute will include and encourages its establishment at the University of California.

label: Segment 13
summary: Requires the Government Operations Agency to submit a report to the Legislature with a framework for the creation and operation of CalCompute, including an analysis of the cost to build CalCompute and recommendations for the governance structure of CalCompute.

label: Segment 14
summary: Requires the consortium to consist of 14 members, including four from academic research institutions and laboratories, and dissolve upon report submission; permits the University of California to receive private donations for implementing CalCompute if it is established there.

label: Segment 15
summary: Adds AI-related terms and definitions, including catastrophic risk and critical safety incident, to the Labor Code.

label: Segment 16
summary: Prohibits frontier developers from restricting or retaliating against covered employee disclosures related to catastrophic risk or violations of the Business and Professions Code; requires  frontier developers to notify all covered employees of their rights and responsibilities.

label: Segment 17
summary: Requires large frontier developers to provide an internal process for covered employees to anonymously disclose information about catastrophic risks or violations of the Business and Professions Code; explains that the disclosures may be shared every quarter with officers of the large frontier developer; allows courts to award attorney fees to plaintiffs who bring successful civil actions for violations of this section.

label: Segment 18
summary: Provides remedies for violations of, and explains how courts should handle cases regarding, this section.

label: Segment 19
summary: Explains the application and enforcement of this chapter and imposes a limitation on the public’s right of access to information regarding safety incident reports, assessments of risks, and reports from covered employees that could threaten public safety or compromise a response to an incident if publicly disclosed.

label: Segment 1
summary: Defines terms for the purposes of this title, including approximate content fingerprint, artificial intelligence, and covered material.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires developers of GenAI models to document covered materials used to train their models, enable the generation of fingerprints compatible with a covered material, and make available a mechanism allowing rights owners to submit a request for information about the developer’s use of covered materials.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires developers to assess fingerprints and provide rights owners with information regarding their use of covered materials within 30 days of an information request; classifies each day after the 30-day period that a developer fails to provide the required information as a discrete violation.

label: Segment 4
summary: Restricts rights owners to one request per quarter to the same developer concerning the same GenAI model, allows rights owners to bring civil actions against developers for non-compliance, and exempts certain GenAI models from the requirements of this title, such as those not trained using covered materials.

label: Segment 1
summary: Declares harms from AI-based chatbots include child grooming, emotional dependency, and distorted social development.

label: Segment 2
summary: Labels significant personalization as harmful for manipulating user engagement, emphasizing risks for children with chatbots.

label: Segment 3
summary: Names the act as the Leading Ethical AI Development for Kids Act; defines key AI-related terms.

label: Segment 4
summary: Prohibits operators from providing harmful companion chatbots to children in specified dangerous scenarios.

label: Segment 5
summary: Allows Attorney General to seek penalties, injunctive relief, or attorney’s fees for Section 22757.22 violations.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires AI model providers to comply with obligations under a Code of Practice and enables AI Office to assess compliance under AI Act.

label: Segment 2
summary: Acknowledges providers' responsibilities for general-purpose AI models and clarifies that disclosure obligations apply only to model modifications or fine-tuning.

label: Segment 3
summary: Clarifies that providers should update documentation to reflect market and technological changes, ensuring information remains useful for compliance and integration, and identifies which AI Act provisions apply to documentation obligations.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires signatories to maintain, update, and share model documentation with the AI Office and downstream providers under Article 53 of the AI Act, to preserve information quality, security, and integrity, with limited exemptions for open-source models.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires signatories to maintain and update model documentation via a model documentation form for AI models, retaining previous versions for 10 years.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires signatories to publicly disclose contact information for the AI Office and downstream providers and provide requested model information pursuant to Articles 91 or 75(3) AI Act.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires signatories to provide AI model information to downstream providers, respecting confidentiality and intellectual property, within 14 days, and encourages public disclosure for transparency.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires signatories to control the quality, integrity, and security of model documentation as evidence of AI Act compliance, following established technical standards and protocols.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Energy to establish a demonstration initiative awarding financial assistance for demonstration projects, of low- to mid-technology readiness, for pipelines and associated infrastructure technologies.

label: Segment 2
summary: Declares advanced sensor technologies such as autonomous sensor systems, including uncrewed aircraft, eligible for financial assistance through the demonstration initiative.

label: Segment 3
summary: Declares advanced computational, data analytics, and machine learning models, as well as autonomous robotic and patch technologies, eligible for financial assistance through the demonstration initiative.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary of Energy to evaluate projects' technical performance and economic feasibility, for return on investment impact, in the selection for the demonstration initiative

label: Segment 1
summary: States that the purpose of stage one is to introduce tools that will help businesses and the government prepare for the implementation of future regulation, and that the use of these tools will enhance human rights protection and minimize AI risks.

label: Segment 2
summary: Advocates for the development of a methodology to assess AI's impact on human rights for regulatory and compliance purposes.

label: Segment 3
summary: Describes the regulatory sandbox the Ministry of Digital Transformation intends to create, proposes the creation of an AI Legal Advisory Platform, and supports voluntary labeling for AI systems to enhance transparency.

label: Segment 4
summary: Encourages clear labeling of AI systems for accountability, transparency, trust, ethical considerations, and regulatory compliance.

label: Segment 5
summary: Explains that the proposed approach to system labeling is voluntary, and that its elements include algorithms, training data, and information for interpretability.

label: Segment 6
summary: Recommends the adoption of soft tools, which are primarily general and sectoral recommendations, before the introduction of legally binding regulation.

label: Segment 7
summary: States that the process of developing general and sectoral AI recommendations has already begun, and that such tools will remain important after the introduction of mandatory regulation.

label: Segment 8
summary: Proposes developing voluntary codes of conduct for AI that businesses can follow, with compliance driven by reputational considerations, not legal enforcement.

label: Segment 9
summary: Proposes establishing a web portal that provides access to AI tools, informs stakeholders, and educates citizens about AI risks.

label: Segment 10
summary: Explains how the proposed soft tools and training tools are expected to have a positive impact on human rights protection.

label: Segment 11
summary: Explains how the proposed tools will positively impact businesses, including by stating that they will reduce the cost of legal support services for the development and implementation of a product.

label: Segment 12
summary: Explains how the proposed tools will positively impact the state, including by stating that they will build the capacity of the future regulatory body.

label: Segment 13
summary: Proposes starting the process of drafting the law immediately after the adoption of the EU AI regulation, and advocates for the partial and gradual implementation of the EU AI regulation and deferring certain provisions in order to provide preparation time.

label: Segment 14
summary: Emphasizes the need for Ukraine to transpose the EU AI Regulation and establish an AI supervisory body before EU accession.

label: Segment 15
summary: Emphasizes the inadequacy of other frameworks as alternatives to EU AI Regulation, and advocates against replacing deferred provisions from EU regulation with similar regulations from other countries.

label: Segment 16
summary: Encourages partnerships with leading AI platforms to address potential human rights violations.

label: Segment 17
summary: Proposes applying the Trusted Flagger concept, which would involve Ukrainian NGOs acting as Trusted Flaggers that monitor and filter complaints about human rights violations related to AI use.

label: Segment 18
summary: Invites feedback on Ukraine's AI regulation approach from government bodies, businesses, academia, and the public sector within 3 months of the publication date.

label: Segment 1
summary: Establishes foundations and principles for AI development and applications in Brazil and guidelines for the promotion and performance of public authorities.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines AI systems as computational processes that, from goals defined by humans, can learn from data to perceive, interpret, and interact with the external environment, excluding automation solely guided by predefined parameters without learning capability.

label: Segment 3
summary: Declares that AI implementation in Brazil is aimed at technological development, sustainable and inclusive economic development, societal well-being, competitiveness, increased participation in the value chain, improved public services, innovation, and environmental protection and preservation.

label: Segment 4
summary: Declares that the foundations for AI development in Brazil are scientific and technological development, free enterprise and competition, human rights and democratic values, freedom of expression and communication, non-discrimination, recognition of the dynamic nature, and the encouragement of self-regulation.

label: Segment 5
summary: Declares that the foundations for AI development in Brazil are based on the protection of privacy, information security, national defence and sovereignty, freedom of business models, preservation of AI systems' security, stability, and functionality, free competition, and freedom from abusive market practices.

label: Segment 6
summary: Declares that the principles for AI development and application in Brazil are beneficial purpose, human centrality, non-discrimination, neutrality, and transparency.

label: Segment 7
summary: Declares that the principles for AI development and application in Brazil are security and prevention, responsible innovation, and data availability.

label: Segment 8
summary: Requires the Brazilian government to observe guidelines on necessity, sector context, specific risks, and proportionality when regulating the implementation of AI.

label: Segment 9
summary: Requires the Brazilian government to observe guidelines on evidence-based decision-making, public consultations, and regulatory impact analysis, as well as subjective liability rules when regulating the implementation of AI.

label: Segment 10
summary: Establishing a tiering of the risk-based management guidelines, encouraging flexible regulatory techniques and responsible innovation in low-risk AI, and granting legal government power to inquire into high-risk AI's security and prevention, and mandating liability for AI-harm in consumer relations and for public service providing legal entities.

label: Segment 11
summary: Established guidelines for the different levels of the Brazilian government's performance regarding the usage and promotion of AI, including promotion of trust in AI, investment in research and development, promotion of interoperability, incentives for adoption, incentives for job market restructuring, and educational innovation.

label: Segment 12
summary: Established guidelines for the different levels of the Brazilian government's performance regarding the usage and promotion of AI, including stimulus for innovation-promoting regulation, stimulus for multi-stakeholder governance collaboration, promotion of international cooperation, as well as transparency and ethical usage.

label: Segment 13
summary: Mandates sectoral bodies and entities to apply the law and to monitor AI risk management, establish rights, duties, and responsibilities, and recognize self-regulatory institutions.

label: Segment 14
summary: Grants exclusive authority to the federal Union for legislating on AI

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes nonexhaustive principles for AI in Brazil rooted in respect for human rights, transparency, and the protection of privacy and personal data.

label: Segment 3
summary: Specifies AI decision-making as auxiliary to humans, establishes that required human supervision will be compatible with the decisions submitted to AI systems, and attributes liability for AI damages to its supervisor.

label: Segment 4
summary: Provides guidelines for promoting educational materials on AI and creating worker protection policies, which apply to the Union, the States, the Federal District, and the Municipalities.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires government AI applications to enhance service quality and efficiency for the population and states that the document goes into effect 45 days after publication.

label: Segment 1
summary: Summarizes Bill 2338 (2023), which addresses the use of Artificial Intelligence.

label: Segment 2
summary: Establishes national AI rules in Brazil to protect rights and ensure safe, reliable systems.

label: Segment 3
summary: Establishes principles for AI in Brazil, emphasizing human rights, equality, innovation, privacy, and education.

label: Segment 4
summary: Establishes principles for AI systems, including transparency, accountability, non-discrimination, human oversight, and risk mitigation.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines terms related to AI systems, providers, operators, and agents for legal purposes.

label: Segment 6
summary: Defines text and data mining as analyzing data to generate information for artificial intelligence system development.

label: Segment 7
summary: Grants rights to AI-affected persons, including information, explanation, and contesting decisions impacting them.

label: Segment 8
summary: Establishes rights to human determination, non-discrimination, privacy, and requires clear AI procedures for exercising these rights.

label: Segment 9
summary: Allows defense of interests and rights under relevant legislation before administrative bodies or courts.

label: Segment 10
summary: Grants affected persons rights to clear information on AI systems, including automated nature and operators' identification.

label: Segment 11
summary: Requires informing people exposed to emotion recognition or biometric categorization systems about their use and operation.

label: Segment 12
summary: Allows affected persons to request explanations about AI decisions or predictions, including rationality, logic, and data usage.

label: Segment 13
summary: Grants individuals the right to contest AI decisions significantly impacting them or based on flawed inferences.

label: Segment 14
summary: Allows individuals to request human review when AI decisions significantly impact their legal interests or rights.

label: Segment 15
summary: Requires significant human involvement in AI-related decisions impacting life or physical integrity of individuals.

label: Segment 16
summary: Prohibits AI system use causing direct, indirect, illegal, or abusive discrimination based on personal characteristics.

label: Segment 17
summary: Requires AI providers to conduct preliminary risk assessments and maintain documentation; mandates impact assessments for high-risk reclassifications.

label: Segment 18
summary: Prohibits AI systems using subliminal techniques or exploiting vulnerabilities to induce harmful behaviors.

label: Segment 19
summary: Restricts use of remote biometric identification in public to specific legal provisions and judicial authorization.

label: Segment 20
summary: Considers AI high-risk when used in critical infrastructure, education, employment, services, credit assessment, emergency response.

label: Segment 21
summary: Regulates AI use in justice, health, autonomous vehicles, biometrics, crime analysis, public security, and migration.

label: Segment 22
summary: Requires the competent authority to update lists of excessive or high-risk AI systems based on specified criteria.

label: Segment 23
summary: Identifies conditions for AI-related risks and requires consultation before updating the risk list.

label: Segment 24
summary: Requires AI agents to ensure system security and rights through transparency and adequate data management measures.

label: Segment 25
summary: Requires privacy-by-design measures, data separation parameters, and information security for AI throughout its life cycle.

label: Segment 26
summary: Requires AI agents operating high-risk systems to document and log operations, and conduct reliability tests.

label: Segment 27
summary: Mandates data management and explainability measures, inclusive team composition, and human oversight for high-risk AI systems.

label: Segment 28
summary: Requires public bodies to adopt measures for high-risk AI use, including consultations, protocols, secure data, and reviews.

label: Segment 29
summary: Requires publicizing AI system assessments by authorities online, regardless of risk. Enacts biometric safeguards law. Prohibits discriminatory data processing.

label: Segment 30
summary: Obligates AI agents to conduct impact assessments for high-risk systems and notify the competent authority.

label: Segment 31
summary: Requires impact assessments to include risk analysis, mitigation steps, monitoring, and transparency for AI systems.

label: Segment 32
summary: Requires algorithmic impact assessments to consider speculative evidence for AI systems with irreversible impacts.

label: Segment 33
summary: Requires continuous, iterative algorithmic impact assessments of high-risk AI systems, with updates regulated by competent authority.

label: Segment 34
summary: Holds AI providers or operators liable for damages, with stricter liability for high-risk systems.

label: Segment 35
summary: Exempts AI agents from liability if not circulated or used for profit, or damage due to others.

label: Segment 36
summary: Allows AI agents to create codes of best practices and governance for organizing, operating, and risk management.

label: Segment 37
summary: Allows AI developers to implement governance programs ensuring compliance and transparency, and adhere to best practice codes.

label: Segment 38
summary: Requires AI agents to report serious security incidents to the competent authority within a reasonable period.

label: Segment 39
summary: Requires the Executive Branch to designate an authority to oversee AI law implementation and enforcement.

label: Segment 40
summary: Coordinates with regulatory authorities to oversee AI sectors, inspect disclosures, and apply sanctions for non-compliance.

label: Segment 41
summary: Designates the competent authority to establish norms and coordinate on AI-related regulatory activities and compliance.

label: Segment 42
summary: Subjects AI agents to fines, warnings, infraction publicity, restrictions, suspensions, and database processing prohibitions.

label: Segment 43
summary: Applies sanctions based on factors, including adopting AI risk minimization measures like algorithmic impact analysis.

label: Segment 44
summary: Allows competent authority to impose preventive measures when AI poses risk of irreparable damage or process ineffectiveness.

label: Segment 45
summary: Requires the competent authority to define and publicly consult procedures for investigating and sanctioning law infractions.

label: Segment 46
summary: Allows the competent authority to authorize experimental regulatory sandboxes for AI innovation meeting specified requirements.

label: Segment 47
summary: Requires competent authority to regulate AI regulatory sandboxes, considering fundamental rights and data protection.

label: Segment 48
summary: Allows AI text and data mining without copyright violation, under conditions restricting reproduction and ensuring security.

label: Segment 49
summary: Requires the competent authority to maintain a public database of high-risk AI impact assessments.

label: Segment 50
summary: Introduces legislative proposals in Brazil to regulate AI principles, guidelines, and applications.

label: Segment 51
summary: Establishes a Commission of Jurists to draft advanced legal text on civil and digital law.

label: Segment 52
summary: Presents a final report and draft bill for AI regulation based on extensive consultations and research.

label: Segment 53
summary: Establishes rights for individuals impacted by AI and provides governance tools for AI oversight and innovation.

label: Segment 54
summary: Establishes risk-based AI regulation prioritizing rights, accountability, and risk management for economic agents.

label: Segment 55
summary: Guarantees rights to contest AI decisions, ensures transparency, and intensifies obligations based on AI's legal impact.

label: Segment 56
summary: Requires preliminary assessment, defines prohibited and high-risk AI applications, and regulates algorithmic impact assessment.

label: Segment 57
summary: Holds providers/operators objectively liable for high-risk AI damages, presumes fault in lesser-risk AI cases. Reinforces discrimination protection.

label: Segment 58
summary: Designates an authority to ensure AI compliance, foster innovation, and outline risk mitigation criteria.

label: Segment 59
summary: Sets guidelines to align copyright and intellectual property rights with treating data as a common good.

label: Segment 1
summary:
Allows the Secretary of Defense to establish AI research institutes focused on national security and defense.

Requires the new institution to work on core AI problems that matter across many defense and national security uses.

Requires the establishment of partnerships across the public, private, and academic sectors.

Requires the new institution to create and grow a research ecosystem to enhance national security and defense capabilities.

Requires the new institution to support workforce development in AI and related disciplines.

label: Segment 2
summary: Authorizes the Secretary of Defense to fund institutes for AI data management, testbeds, research, and outreach.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to award financial assistance domestically for AI affecting national security.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines "eligible host institution" as certain US higher education institutions or senior military colleges.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to integrate commercial AI capabilities for logistics in 2026 exercises.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to identify AI products addressing logistics with specified security and adaptability.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to expedite approvals for secure data use by commercial AI providers, including compliance with applicable cybersecurity policies and verification of measures.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to brief Congress on AI capabilities integrated into military exercises.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires combatant command commander to brief Congress on commercial AI integration, effects, and future recommendations.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of the Navy to develop and implement a pilot program for AI-driven immersive training and assessment.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Secretary of the Navy to report on generative AI training pilot program results within 90 days; terminates the program one year after the date of establishment.

label: Segment 1
summary: Encourages the use of AI to facilitate Department of Defense financial statement audits for FY 2026.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Chief Digital and AI Office Director, in coordination with the Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering and the Inspector General of the Department, to oversee AI adoption in financial management and business operations.

label: Segment 1
summary:
Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish an AI sandbox task force for the development and deployment of AI sandbox environments necessary to support 
artificial intelligence experimentation, training, familiarization, and 
development across the Department of Defense.

label: Segment 2
summary: Designates the Chief Digital and AI Officer as chair of the Department of Defense AI Task Force; includes key officials from the Department of Defense, military departments, and combatant commands.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Task Force to standardize AI sandbox requirements across the Department of Defense, catalog and align existing solutions, improve efficiency and approvals, and provide clear guidance and resources to support users of all technical skill levels.

label: Segment 4
summary: Defines artificial intelligence terms, including 'artificial intelligence sandbox environment' and 'authority to operate'.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish the Artificial Intelligence Futures Steering Committee by April 1, 2026.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the steering committee to formulate policies for the Department of Defense's AI usage and analyze AI development trajectories.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the committee to assess adversaries' AI trajectories and analyze efects of integrating AI into DoD systems and networks.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the committee to develop a strategy that considers threats for AI governance, adoption, and oversight, as well as analyze the AI adversarial threat landscape.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Steering Committee to meet at least once every three months and the Deputy Secretary of Defense to report the committee's findings to congress by January 31, 2027.

label: Segment 6
summary: Defines "artificial intelligence" and "innovation ecosystem" for purposes of the section, which terminates December 31, 2027.

label: Segment 1
summary: Limits AI research funding within the National Nuclear Security Administration to supporting nuclear security missions.

label: Segment 2
summary: Allows establishment of an enduring AI research program in agencies beyond the Department of Energy Administration.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Department of Defense, within one year of enactment, to establish rules ensuring that biological data produced by DoD-funded research is collected and stored in a manner that facilitates the use by advanced computational methods, including AI.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines requirements for biological data, including quality metrics, cybersecurity, privacy, and exceptions for national security.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary to consult with military, private, and academic entities about the requirements and review existing Federal frameworks for the use of biological data resources for advanced computational method.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop and implement a cybersecurity policy for AI and ML systems.

label: Segment 2
summary: Specifies AI security measures, including threat protection, cybersecurity, frameworks, governance standards, and workforce training.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to review and report on AI/ML cybersecurity and governance practices.

label: Segment 4
summary: Analyzes AI security management for defense, evaluates policy alignment, recommends security enhancements, identifies additional legislative needs.

label: Segment 5
summary: References definitions of "artificial intelligence" and "machine learning" in the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act 2020.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to develop a security framework for AI and machine learning technologies to mitigate risks from their deployment.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires that the framework covers all relevant security aspects of the Department of Defense's AI systems.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires a risk-based framework using existing cybersecurity documents to secure AI technology against national security risks.

label: Segment 4
summary: Requires the framework to prioritize security for AI systems of high interest to cyber threat actors.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to amend regulations for AI entities to implement best practices.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to balance AI development speed with national security risk mitigation.

label: Segment 7
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to collaborate with industry and academia on AI framework development and establishes an implementation plan for the framework.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines AI, "covered AI and machine learning technology," and "covered entity."

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to revise mandatory cybersecurity training to include AI-related cybersecurity challenges within one year.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense and contractors to exclude and prohibit the use of certain artificial intelligence systems in defense systems and contracts based on national security risk determinations, and directs department-wide governance guidance for such exclusions.

label: Segment 2
summary: Allows the Secretary of Defense to grant case-by-case waivers permitting otherwise prohibited use of covered artificial intelligence for research, national security, military, or mission-critical purposes, and to take steps to mitigate risks arising from such waivers.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines “artificial intelligence,” “covered artificial intelligence,” and “covered artificial intelligence companies,” including AI developed by specified foreign-linked entities and entities subject to foreign ownership or control.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to establish a Cross-Functional Team to develop an assessment framework and governance structure for evaluation of AI models deployed by the Department of Defense.

label: Segment 2
summary: Designates the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer as head of the cross-functional team and requires the Secretary of Defense to include representatives from specified Department of Defense offices, services, and commands.

label: Segment 3
summary: Requires the Cross-Functional Team to develop assessment frameworks for current AI models, Department of Defense (DOD)–wide guidelines for future models, governance structures, and collaboration mechanisms for the development, testing, and assessment of AI models used by the DOD.

label: Segment 4
summary: Specifies mandatory elements of the Department of Defense’s standardized AI assessment framework, including performance standards, testing and validation procedures, ethical and security compliance requirements, and use of external agency assessments.

label: Segment 5
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to designate Department of Defense organizations as functional leads for AI applications based on expertise and capacity, and assigns the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer responsibility for Department-wide guidance on AI business systems and commercial AI models.

label: Segment 6
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to assess all major AI systems using a standardized framework by specified deadlines and provide briefings on progress to the congressional defense committees.

label: Segment 7
summary: Terminates the Cross-Functional Team by 2030 and requires succession planning and reporting by the Secretary of Defense.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines "artificial intelligence", "functional area" among others like "major artificial intelligence system" which means AI impacting high-impact information systems or involving 500+ Department of Defense (DOD) users and used for functions like military operations, business operations etc.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Director of the NSA to develop AI security guidance against nation-state adversaries.

label: Segment 2
summary: Allows the NSA Director to collaborate on AI model safety and publish related security guidance.

label: Segment 1
summary: Renames intelligence community officials as Chief Artificial Intelligence Officers by amending Section 6702 of the Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2023.

label: Segment 2
summary: Directs the intelligence community to identify and share reusable AI systems and functions among its elements no later than one year after the enactment of this Act.

label: Segment 3
summary: Instructs the intelligence community to adopt model contract terms for AI data rights and track and evaluate the performance of AI systems.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Director to apply AI policies of the intelligence community to publicly available models hosted in classified environments.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires the Chief AI Officer to establish AI model testing standards for risk, performance benchmarks, efficacy, safety, fairness, transparency, accountability, trustworthiness across ML, object detection and recognition use-cases; requires the Officer to set standards for a secure computing environment for model testing and evaluation prior to acquisition.

label: Segment 3
summary: Prohibits intelligence officers from directing vendors to alter AI models to favor specific viewpoints, and clarifies the restriction applies only within the intelligence community.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires Director of National Intelligence to develop standards and guidelines for removing Deepseek from intelligence community systems by their operators.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires for the standards and guidelines removing the use of Deepsake in intelligence community systems to include national security and research exceptions; requires the Director of National Intelligence to develop the initial standards and guidelines within 60 days.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of the Air Force to submit an ISR capabilities roadmap by October 1, 2026.

label: Segment 2
summary: Requires a strategic assessment and inventory of current and projected intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance requirements.

label: Segment 3
summary: Outlines planned investments in AI, machine learning, and autonomous platforms for intelligence and reconnaissance.

label: Segment 4
summary: Assesses intelligence data integration and risks, describes Air Force roles, and outlines roadmap timeline and milestones.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to ensure maintenance includes technical data or diagnostic tools unless unavailable.

label: Segment 2
summary: Defines "diagnostic tool" as using AI and machine learning for predictive maintenance in detecting equipment issues.

label: Segment 1
summary: Requires the Secretary, in consultation with DoD's AI Officer, to establish an AI-based vehicle maintenance pilot program.

label: Segment 2

label: Segment 1

label: Segment 1
summary: Establishes protections for individual rights concerning computational algorithms through the Artificial Intelligence Civil Rights Act of 2025.

label: Segment 2
summary: Lists sections addressing AI civil rights, algorithm standards, transparency, enforcement, and federal resources.

label: Segment 3
summary: Defines "commercial act" as actions involving a covered algorithm for monetary or valuable consideration.

summary: Defines "consequential action" as acts affecting access, security, or conditions in sectors like employment, education, and health.

label: Segment 5
summary: Defines "covered algorithm" as computational processes, including AI techniques, affecting consequential actions, or deemed appropriate by the Commission.

label: Segment 7
summary: Defines "deployer" and "developer" roles and analyzes disparate impact of algorithms in commercial or government contexts.

label: Segment 8
summary: Defines "harm" as a non-trivial adverse effect, and "independent auditor" as impartial evaluator of AI algorithms.

label: Segment 9
summary: Defines personal data as information linked to an individual, excluding de-identified data.

label: Segment 10
summary: Defines "protected characteristic" as specific personal traits, including race, sex, disability, and genetic information.

label: Segment 11
summary: Defines "public accommodation" as businesses, parks, transportation, and facilities open to the public, excluding private clubs.

label: Segment 12
summary: Defines 'State data protection authority' as supervising State data protection and handling legal complaints.

label: Segment 13
summary: Prohibits developers from using algorithms causing disparate impacts or discrimination, except for testing or research purposes.

label: Segment 14
summary: Requires developers and deployers to conduct pre-deployment evaluations of algorithms for potential harm.

label: Segment 15
summary: Requires developers to engage an independent auditor for a comprehensive pre-deployment evaluation if potential harm is plausible.

label: Segment 16
summary: Requires developers to document algorithm outputs, stakeholder consultations, protected characteristics, data sources, and training processes.

label: Segment 17
summary: Requires assessing AI algorithms for harm and disparate impacts, with mitigation recommendations and monitoring guidelines.

label: Segment 18
summary: Requires deployers to engage independent auditors for pre-deployment evaluations if potential harms are plausible.

label: Segment 19

label: Segment 20

label: Segment 21

label: Segment 22
summary: Requires deployers to conduct and submit annual impact assessments of covered algorithms to the Commission.

label: Segment 23
summary: Requires deployers to engage independent auditors for full impact assessments if algorithms cause harm.

label: Segment 24
summary: Requires detailed reporting on algorithm outputs, usage, harm prevention, and disparate impacts as the Commission prescribes.

label: Segment 25
summary: Requires independent auditor to report impact assessment to deployer; deployer summarizes report for developer.

label: Segment 26
summary: Requires developers to annually review deployer-submitted impact assessments on algorithm usage, data, and contractual compliance.

label: Segment 27
summary: Requires evaluation of covered algorithms for performance, harm, disparate impacts, possible modifications, and other safety actions.

label: Segment 28
summary: Requires developers and deployers to report, retain, and publish AI evaluations while protecting trade secrets and privacy.

label: Segment 29
summary: Requires the Commission to create rules for AI evaluations, impact assessments, privacy, and information sharing.

label: Segment 30
summary: Requires developers or deployers to evaluate, certify, and consult on covered algorithm impacts and mitigate identified harms.

label: Segment 31
summary: Requires developers and deployers to ensure algorithms perform reasonably and truthfully according to advertised standards.

label: Segment 32
summary: Requires developers to share compliance information and cooperate with or arrange assessments for deployers.

label: Segment 33
summary: Requires contracts between developers and deployers to specify data handling, deployment instructions, and parties' rights.

label: Segment 34
summary: Prohibits developers and deployers from combining data and requires contract retention for 10 years.

label: Segment 35
summary: Requires the Commission to regulate human opt-out options for consequential algorithmic actions within two years.

label: Segment 36
summary: Prohibits AI developers from misleading statements or interface designs that impair individuals' rights or autonomy.

label: Segment 37
summary: Requires the Commission to regulate appeals for consequential actions from deployers' covered algorithm use.

label: Segment 38
summary: Prohibits developers or deployers from retaliating against individuals for exercising rights or reporting violations.

label: Segment 39
summary: Requires developers or deployers to publicly disclose practices, identity, contact, data processing, and data transfers.

label: Segment 40
summary: Requires disclosures about AI safety, compliance practices, and rights, accessible in multiple languages and for disabilities.

label: Segment 41
summary: Requires developers or deployers to notify individuals about material changes to AI disclosure before implementation.

label: Segment 42
summary: Requires a deployer to provide a concise public notice about a covered algorithm, accessible and under 500 words.

label: Segment 43
summary: Requires deployers to provide short-form notifications and establishes reporting mechanisms for algorithm impact violations.

label: Segment 44
summary: Requires the Commission to study the feasibility of requiring deployers to explain covered algorithm impacts.

label: Segment 45
summary: Requires developers to provide deployers information ensuring viable explanations to individuals upon request.

label: Segment 46
summary: Requires the Commission to publish and update a webpage on consumer rights under the Act.

label: Segment 47
summary: Requires the Commission to publish annual online reports on algorithm impact assessments, trends, and recommendations.

label: Segment 48
summary: Requires the Commission to develop a public repository for AI evaluations, impact assessments, and developer reviews.

label: Segment 49
summary: Requires the Commission to publish evaluations and assessments, with possible redactions for trade secrets and personal data.

label: Segment 50
summary: Empowers the Federal Trade Commission to enforce this Act and develop rules under its authority.

label: Segment 51
summary: Requires the Federal Trade Commission to enforce this Act against specified non-profit and regulated entities.

label: Segment 52
summary: Allows state attorneys general or data protection authorities to enforce the Act through civil actions in federal court.

label: Segment 53
summary: Requires state attorneys general to notify the Commission before filing civil actions, allowing Commission intervention.

label: Segment 54
summary: Allows state attorneys general to use state laws for investigations and evidence collection. Requires the Commission to establish coordination procedures with federal agencies, including information sharing.

label: Segment 55
summary: Allows individuals to bring civil actions for violations, with potential awards including treble damages and attorney's fees.

label: Segment 56
summary: Allows the Commission and state attorneys general to intervene in civil actions within 60 days.

label: Segment 57
summary: Invalidates pre-dispute arbitration agreements and joint action waivers related to disputes under this Act.

label: Segment 58
summary: States that invalidation of one provision does not affect the validity of the rest of the Act.

label: Segment 59
summary: Clarifies that this Act does not reduce existing obligations regarding collective bargaining, safety, or employee rights.

label: Segment 60
summary: Requires the OPM Director to establish an occupational series for federal positions in algorithm auditing.

label: Segment 61
summary: Authorizes the Commission to hire 500 personnel for unfair practices related to algorithm development or deployment.
